frontmatter.title: Appendix C: R Reference
frontmatter.subtitle: MATH 4210 -- setup and every R function used, by chapter

nameParsed.literal: Anjana Yatawara, Department of Mathematics, CSU Bakersfield

authors.name: Anjana Yatawara, Department of Mathematics, CSU Bakersfield

content.name: Creative Commons Attribution Share Alike 4.0 International

children.value:
This appendix is a lookup table, not a tutorial. Use it to install R and the course
packages once, to find the one-line syntax for reading a course dataset, and to look up any
R function you saw in a chapter but forgot how it works. Functions are grouped by the
chapter that introduces them; a function used again in a later chapter is not repeated,
so if you cannot find something under “your” chapter, check an earlier one.
The course was built and checked against
for your operating system, then install

as your editor; it is not
required, but it makes running the chapter code much easier.
Every dataset in this book loads with base R alone (

present; the line above is safe to run regardless. Load a package in any R session with

before using its functions; the chapter tables below note which
package each function comes from when it is not base R.
at the console; it should report

; if either errors with “there is no package called”,
re-run the
Every dataset used in this book lives in

as a plain CSV, one row per observation,
with column headers already set to the names used in the chapters. Read any of them with
base R’s
, giving the path relative to the book’s root folder:

All chapter R code assumes your working directory is the book’s root folder (the one that
contains
call in the book uses a path like

,
never a full file-system path. In RStudio, open the project at the book root, or set it
by hand with
before running any chapter’s code. See
Appendix E: The Datasets of This Book
for the story, provenance, and variable list of every dataset.
How to read the tables below

children.label: How to read the tables below

children.value:
Each row gives the function, the package it comes from (blank means base R, already loaded
in any session), a one-line description of what it does, and a minimal example. Functions
are listed once, in the chapter where the book first uses them; later chapters build on
these without repeating the entry.

itself is not tabulated; load whichever
package a function’s row names before calling it.
Chapter 1: Regression as a way of thinking

children.label: Chapter 1: Regression as a way of thinking

children.value: Read a CSV file into a data frame.
Fit a linear model by least squares.
Extract fitted coefficients from a model.
Compute fitted or predicted values from a model.
Number of rows in a data frame.
Apply a function to each element of a list or vector, simplifying the result.
Round a number to a given number of decimal places.
Draw a scatterplot or other base-R plot.
Add a straight line to an existing plot.
Get or set plotting parameters (layout, margins).
Build a data frame from named vectors.

Chapter 14: Poisson regression and the GLM idea

children.label: Chapter 14: Poisson regression and the GLM idea

children.value: glm(count ~ x, data = d, family = poisson)
Family object for overdispersed count data; same mean model, inflated variance.
